text: The colon is visibly rolling away from its initial position during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a stapling technique on the ileocolic junction, using a surgical stapler to secure the tissue.
The surgeon elevates the colon, which rolls away at an angle, causing a non-perpendicular staple firing. The surgeon prepares to readjust the stapler and elevate the bowel to ensure proper alignment.
The surgeon adjusts the positioning to ensure the colon falls away properly, preventing issues with staple alignment during the ileocolic resection.
The surgeon achieves a perpendicular staple line with good perfusion to the planned anastomosis site, ensuring optimal alignment and blood flow.
The surgeon checks the tip of the colon to ensure it has fallen down in a perpendicular orientation for accurate stapling.
The surgeon aligns the stapler with the colon and successfully fires it, ensuring a perpendicular staple line without any complications.
The surgeon performs a second firing of the stapler due to the length of the bowel, ensuring a flat staple line across the tissue.
The surgeon positions the stapler perpendicular to the original staple line, ensuring alignment for the next step in the ileocolic resection.
The surgeon maintains a perpendicular common staple line on the colon, ensuring it does not curve or narrow the lumen.
